author: Augustine of Hippo
book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.

ccel.title: NPNF1-08. St. Augustine: Exposition on the Book of Psalms

source.title: NPNF1-08. St. Augustine: Exposition on the Book of Psalms

title: Psalm VII

source.title: NPNF1-08. St. Augustine: Exposition on the Book of Psalms

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: A psalm to David himself, which he sung to the Lord, for the words of Chusi, son of Jemini.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Now the story which gave occasion to this prophecy may be easily recognised in the second book of Kings.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For there Chusi, the friend of king David, went over to the side of Abessalon, his son, who was carrying on war against his father, for the purpose of discovering and reporting the designs which he was taking against his father, at the instigation of Achitophel, who had revolted from David’s friendship, and was instructing by his counsel, to the best of his power, the son against the father.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But since it is not the story itself which is to be the subject of consideration in this Psalm, from which the prophet hath taken a veil of mysteries, if we have passed over to Christ, let the veil be taken away.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And first let us inquire into the signification of the very names, what it means.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For there have not been wanting interpreters, who investigating these same words, not carnally according to the letter, but spiritually, declare to us that Chusi should be interpreted silence; and Gemini, right-handed; Achitophel, brother’s ruin.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Among which interpretations, Judas, that traitor, again meets us, that Abessalon should bear his image, according to that interpretation of it as a father’s peace; in that his father was full of thoughts of peace toward him: although he in his guile had war in his heart, as was treated of in the third Psalm.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Now as we find in the Gospels that the disciples of our Lord Jesus Christ are called sons, so in the same Gospels we find they are called brethren also.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For the Lord on the resurrection saith, “Go and say to My brethren.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And the Apostle calls Him “the first begotten among many brethren.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: The ruin then of that disciple, who betrayed Him, is rightly understood to be a brother’s ruin, which we said is the interpretation of Achitophel.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Now as to Chusi, from the interpretation of silence, it is rightly understood that our Lord contended against that guile in silence, that is, in that most deep secret, whereby “blindness happened in part to Israel,” when they were persecuting the Lord, that the fulness of the Gentiles might enter in, and “so all Israel might be saved.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: When the Apostle came to this profound secret and deep silence, he exclaimed, as if struck with a kind of awe of its very depth, “O the depth of the riches of the wisdom and knowledge of God! how unsearchable are His judgments, and His ways past finding out!

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For who hath known the wind of the Lord, or who hath been His counsellor?”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Thus that great silence he does not so much discover by explanation, as he sets forth its greatness in admiration.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: In this silence the Lord, hiding the sacrament of His adorable passion, turns the brother’s voluntary ruin, that is, His betrayer’s impious wickedness, into the order of His mercy and providence: that what he with perverse mind wrought for one Man’s destruction, He might by providential overruling dispose for all men’s salvation.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: The perfect soul then, which is already worthy to know the secret of God, sings a Psalm unto the Lord, she sings “for the words of Chusi,” because she has attained to know the words of that silence: for among unbelievers and persecutors there is that silence and secret.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But among His own, to whom it is said, “Now I call you no more servants; for the servant knoweth not what his lord doeth; but I have called you friends, for all things that I have heard of My Father I have made known unto you: among His friends, I say, there is not the silence, but the words of the silence, that is, the meaning of that silence set forth and manifested.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Which silence, that is, Chusi, is called the son of Gemini, that is, righthanded.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For what was done for the Saints was not to be hidden from them.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And yet He saith, “Let not the left hand know what the right hand doeth.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: The perfect soul then, to which that secret has been made known, sings in prophecy “for the words of Chusi,” that is, for the knowledge of that same secret.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Which secret God at her right hand, that is, favourable and propitious unto her, has wrought.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Wherefore this silence is called the Son of the right hand, which is, “Chusi, the son of Gemini.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “O Lord my God, in Thee have I hoped: save me from all them that persecute me, and deliver me” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: As one to whom, already perfected, all the war and enmity of vice being overcome, there remaineth no enemy but the envious devil, he says, “Save me from all them that persecute me, and deliver me (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: 2): lest at any time he tear my soul as a lion.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: The Apostle says, “Your adversary the devil, as a roaring lion, walketh about, seeking whom he may devour.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Therefore when the Psalmist said in the plural number, “Save me from all them that persecute me:” he afterwards introduced the singular, saying, “lest at any time he tear my soul as a lion.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For he does not say, lest at any time they tear: he knew what enemy and violent adversary of the perfect soul remained.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Whilst there be none to redeem, nor to save:” that is, lest he tear me, whilst Thou redeemest not, nor savest.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For, if God redeem not, nor save, he tears.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And that it might be clear that the already perfect soul, which is to be on her guard against the most insidious snares of the devil only, says this, see what follows.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “O Lord my God, if I have done this” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: What is it that he calls “this”?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Since he does not mention the sin by name, are we to understand sin generally?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: If this sense displease us, we may take that to be meant which follows: as if we had asked, what is this that you say, “this”?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: He answers, “If there be iniquity in my hands.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Now then it is clear that it is said of all sin, “If I have repaid them that recompense me evil” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Which none can say with truth, but the perfect.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For so the Lord says, “Be ye perfect, as your Father which is in heaven; who maketh His sun to rise upon the good and the evil, and raineth on the just and the unjust.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: He then who repayeth not them that recompense evil, is perfect.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: When therefore the perfect soul prays “for the words of Chusi, the son of Jemini,” that is, for the knowledge of that secret and silence, which the Lord, favourable to us and merciful, wrought for our salvation, so as to endure, and with all patience bear, the guiles of this betrayer: as if He should say to this perfect soul, explaining the design of this secret, For thee ungodly and a sinner, that thine iniquities might be washed away by My blood-shedding, in great silence and great patience I bore with My betrayer; wilt not thou imitate me, that thou too mayest not repay evil for evil?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Considering then, and understanding what the Lord has done for him, and by His example going on to perfection, the Psalmist says, “If I have repaid them that recompense me evil:” that is, if I have not done what Thou hast taught me by Thy example: “may I therefore fall by mine enemies empty.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And he says well, not, If I have repaid them that do me evil; but, who “recompense.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For who so recompenseth, had received somewhat already.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Now it is an instance of greater patience, not even to repay him evil, who after receiving benefits returns evil for good, than if without receiving any previous benefit he had had a mind to injure.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: If therefore he says, “I have repaid them that recompense me evil:” that is, If I have not imitated Thee in that silence, that is, in Thy patience, which Thou hast wrought for me, “may I fall by mine enemies empty.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For he is an empty boaster, who, being himself a man, desires to avenge himself on a man; and whilst he openly seeks to overcome a man, is secretly himself overcome by the devil, rendered empty by vain and proud joy, because he could not, as it were, be conquered.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: The Psalmist knows then where a greater victory may be obtained, and where “the Father which seeth in secret will reward.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Lest then he repay them that recompense evil, he overcomes his anger rather than another man, being instructed too by those writings, wherein it is written, “Better is he that overcometh his anger, than he that taketh a city.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “If I have repaid them that recompense me evil, may I therefore fall by my enemies empty.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: He seems to swear by way of execration, which is the heaviest kind of oath, as when one says, If I have done so and so, may I suffer so and so.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But swearing in a swearer’s mouth is one thing, in a prophet’s meaning another.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For here he mentions what will really befall men who repay them that recompense evil; not what, as by an oath, he would imprecate on himself or any other.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Let the enemy” therefore “persecute my soul and take it” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: By again naming the enemy in the singular number, he more and more clearly points out him whom he spoke of above as a lion.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For he persecutes the soul, and if he has deceived it, will take it.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For the limit of men’s rage is the destruction of the body; but the soul, after this visible death, they cannot keep in their power: whereas whatever souls the devil shall have taken by his persecutions, he will keep.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “And let him tread my life upon the earth:” that is, by treading let him make my life earth, that is to say, his food.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For he is not only called a lion, but a serpent too, to whom it was said, “Earth shalt thou eat.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And to the sinner was it said, “Earth thou art, and into earth shalt thou go.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “And let him bring down my glory to the dust.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: This is that dust which “the wind casteth forth from the face of the earth,” to wit, vain and silly boasting of the proud, puffed up, not of solid weight, as a cloud of dust carried away by the wind.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Justly then has he here spoken of the glory, which he would not have brought down to dust.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For he would have it solidly established in conscience before God, where there is no boasting.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “He that glorieth,” saith the Apostle, “let him glory in the Lord.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: This solidity is brought down to the dust if one through pride despising the secrecy of conscience, where God only proves a man, desires to glory before men.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Hence comes what the Psalmist elsewhere says, “God shall bruise the bones of them that please men.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Now he that has well learnt or experienced the steps in overcoming vices, knows that this vice of empty glory is either alone, or more than all, to be shunned by the perfect.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For that by which the soul first fell, she overcomes the last.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “For the beginning of all sin is pride:” and again, “The beginning of man’s pride is to depart from God.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Arise, O Lord, in Thine anger” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Why yet does he, who we say is perfect, incite God to anger?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Must we not see, whether he rather be not perfect, who, when he was being stoned, said, “O Lord, lay not this sin to their charge”?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Or does the Psalmist pray thus not against men, but against the devil and his angels, whose possession sinners and the ungodly are?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: He then does not pray against him in wrath, but in mercy, whosoever prays that that possession may be taken from him by that Lord “who justifieth the ungodly.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For when the ungodly is justified, from ungodly he is made just, and from being the possession of the devil he passes into the temple of God.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And since it is a punishment that a possession, in which one longs to have rule, should be taken away from him: this punishment, that he should cease to possess those whom he now possesses, the Psalmist calls the anger of God against the devil.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Arise, O Lord; in Thine anger.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Arise” (he has used it as “appear”), in words, that is, human and obscure; as though God sleeps, when He is unrecognised and hidden in His secret workings.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Be exalted in the borders of mine enemies.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: He means by borders the possession itself, in which he wishes that God should be exalted, that is, be honoured and glorified, rather than the devil, while the ungodly are justified and praise God.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “And arise, O Lord my God, in the commandment that Thou hast given:” that is, since Thou hast enjoined humility, appear in humility; and first fulfil what Thou hast enjoined; that men by Thy example overcoming pride may not be possessed of the devil, who against Thy commandments advised to pride, saying, “Eat, and your eyes shall be opened, and ye shall be as gods.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “And the congregation of the people shall surround Thee.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: This may be understood two ways.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For the congregation of the people can be taken, either of them that believe, or of them that persecute, both of which took place in the same humiliation of our Lord: in contempt of which the multitude of them that persecute surrounded Him; concerning which it is said, “Why have the heathen raged, and the people meditated vain things?”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But of them that believe through His humiliation the multitude so surrounded Him, that it could be said with the greatest truth, “blindness in part is happened unto Israel, that the fulness of the Gentiles might come in:” and again, “Ask of me, and I will give Thee the Gentiles for Thine inheritance, and the boundaries of the earth for Thy possession.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “And for their sakes return Thou on high:” that is, for the sake of this congregation return Thou on high: which He is understood to have done by His resurrection and ascension into heaven.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For being thus glorified He gave the Holy Ghost, which before His exaltation could not be given, as it is written in the Gospel, “for the Holy Ghost was not yet given, because that Jesus was not yet glorified.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Having then returned on high for the sake of the congregation of the people, He sent the Holy Ghost: by whom the preachers of the Gospel being filled, filled the whole world with Churches.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: It can be taken also in this sense: “Arise, O Lord, in Thine anger, and be exalted in the borders of mine enemies:” that is, arise in Thine anger, and let not mine enemies understand Thee; so that to “be exalted,” should be this, become high, that Thou mayest not be understood; which has reference to the silence spoken of above.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For it is of this exaltation thus said in another Psalm, “And He ascended upon Cherubim, and flew:” and, “He made darkness His secret place.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: In which exaltation, or concealment, when for their sins’ desert they shall not understand Thee, who shall crucify Thee, “the congregation” of believers “shall surround Thee.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For in His very humiliation He was exalted, that is, was not understood.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: So that, “And arise, O Lord my God, in the commandment that Thou hast given:” may have reference to this, that is, when Thou showest Thyself, be high or deep that mine enemies may not understand Thee.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Now sinners are the enemies of the just man, and the ungodly of the godly man.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “And the congregation of the people shall surround Thee:” that is, by this very circumstance, that those who crucify Thee understand Thee not, the Gentiles shall believe on Thee, and so “shall the congregation of the people surround Thee.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But what follows, if this be the true meaning, has in it more pain, that it begins already to be perceived, than joy that it is understood.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For it follows, “and for their sakes return Thou on high,” that is, and for the sake of this congregation of the human race, wherewith the Churches are crowded, return Thou on high, that is, again cease to be understood.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: What then is, “and for their sakes,” but that this congregation too will offend Thee, so that Thou mayest most truly foretell and say, “Thinkest Thou when the Son of man shall come, He will find faith on the earth?”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Again, of the false prophets, who are understood to be heretics, He says, “Because of their iniquity the love of many shall wax cold.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Since then even in the Churches, that is, in that congregation of peoples and nations, where the Christian name has most widely spread, there shall be so great abundance of sinners, which is already, in great measure, perceived; is not that famine of the word here predicted, which has been threatened by another prophet also?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Is it not too for this congregation’s sake, who, by their sins, are estranging from themselves that light of truth, that God returns on high, that is, so that faith, pure and cleansed from the corruption of all perverse opinions, is held and received, either not at all, or by the very few of whom it was said, “Blessed is he that shall endure to the end, the same shall be saved”?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Not without cause then is it said, “and for the sake of this” congregation “return Thou on high:” that is, again withdraw into the depth of Thy secrecy, even for the sake of this congregation of the peoples, that hath Thy name, and doeth not Thy deeds.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But whether the former exposition of this place, or this last be the more suitable, without prejudice to any one better, or equal, or as good, it follows very consistently, “the Lord judgeth the people.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For whether He returned on high, when, after the resurrection, He ascended into heaven, well does it follow, “The Lord judgeth the people:” for that He will come from thence to judge the quick and the dead.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Or whether He return on high, when the understanding of the truth leaves sinful Christians, for that of His coming it has been said, “Thinkest thou the Son of Man on His coming will find faith on the earth?”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “The Lord” then “judgeth the people.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: What Lord, but Jesus Christ?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “For the Father judgeth no man, but hath committed all judgment unto the Son.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Wherefore this soul which prayeth perfectly, see how she fears not the day of judgment, and with a truly secure longing says in her prayer, “Thy kingdom come: judge me,” she says, “O Lord, according to my righteousness.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: In the former Psalm a weak one was entreating, imploring rather the mercy of God, than mentioning any desert of his own: since the Son of God came “to call sinners to repentance.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Therefore he had there said, “Save me, O Lord, for Thy mercy’s sake;” that is, not for my desert’s sake.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But now, since being called he hath held and kept the commandments which he received, he is bold to say, “Judge me, O Lord, according to my righteousness, and according to my harmlessness, that is upon me.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: This is true harmlessness, which harms not even an enemy.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Accordingly, well does he require to be judged according to his harmlessness, who could say with truth, “If I have repaid them that recompense me evil.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: As for what he added, “that is upon me,” it can refer not only to harmlessness, but can be understood also with reference to righteousness; that the sense should be this, Judge me, O Lord, according to my righteousness, and according to my harmlessness, which righteousness and harmlessness is upon me.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: By which addition he shows that this very thing, that the soul is righteous and harmless, she has not by herself, but by God who giveth brightness and light.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For of this he says in another Psalm, “Thou, O Lord, wilt light my candle.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And of John it is said, that “he was not the light, but bore witness of the light.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “He was a burning and shining candle.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: That light then, whence souls, as candles, are kindled, shines forth not with borrowed, but with original, brightness, which light is truth itself.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: It is then so said, “According to my righteousness, and according to my harmlessness, that is upon me,” as if a burning and shining candle should say, Judge me according to the flame which is upon me, that is, not that wherewith I am myself, but that whereby I shine enkindled of thee.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “But let the wickedness of sinners be consummated” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: He says, “be consummated,” be completed, according to that in the Apocalypse, “Let the righteous become more righteous, and let the filthy be filthy still.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For the wickedness of those men appears consummate, who crucified the Son of God; but greater is theirs who will not live uprightly, and hate the precepts of truth, for whom the Son of God was crucified.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Let the wickedness of sinners,” then he says, “be consummated,” that is, arrive at the height of wickedness, that just judgment may be able to come at once.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But since it is not only said, “Let the filthy be filthy still;” but it is said also, “Let the righteous become more righteous;” he joins on the words, “And Thou shalt direct the righteous, O God, who searcheth the hearts and reins.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: How then can the righteous be directed but in secret? when even by means of those things which, in the commencement of the Christian ages, when as yet the saints were oppressed by the persecution of the men of this world, appeared marvellous to men, now that the Christian name has begun to be in such high dignity, hypocrisy, that is pretence, has increased; of those, I mean, who by the Christian profession had rather please men than God.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: How then is the righteous man directed in so great confusion of pretence, save whilst God searcheth the hearts and reins; seeing all men’s thoughts, which are meant by the word heart; and their delights, which are understood by the word reins?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For the delight in things temporal and earthly is rightly ascribed to the reins; for that it is both the lower part of man, and that region where the pleasure of carnal generation dwells, through which man’s nature is transferred into this life of care, and deceiving joy, by the succession of the race.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: God then, searching our heart, and perceiving that it is there where our treasure is, that is, in heaven; searching also the reins, and perceiving that we do not assent to flesh and blood, but delight ourselves in the Lord, directs the righteous man in his inward conscience before Him, where no man seeth, but He alone who perceiveth what each man thinketh, and what delighteth each.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For delight is the end of care; because to this end does each man strive by care and thought, that he may attain to his delight.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: He therefore seeth our cares, who searcheth the heart.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: He seeth too the ends of cares, that is delights, who narrowly searcheth the reins; that when He shall find that our cares incline neither to the lust of the flesh, nor to the lust of the eyes, nor to the pride of life, all which pass away as a shadow, but that they are raised upward to the joys of things eternal, which are spoilt by no change, He may direct the righteous, even He, the God who searcheth the hearts and reins.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For our works, which we do in deeds and words, may be known unto men; but with what mind they are done, and to what end we would attain by means of them, He alone knoweth, the God who searcheth the hearts and reins.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “My righteous help is from the Lord, who maketh whole the upright in heart” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: The offices of medicine are twofold, on the curing infirmity, the other the preserving health.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: According to the first it was said in the preceding Psalm, “Have mercy on me, O Lord, for I am weak;” according to the second it is said in this Psalm, “If there be iniquity in my hands, if I have repaid them that recompense me evil, may I therefore fall by my enemies empty.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For there the weak prays that he may be delivered, here one already whole that he may not change for the worse.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: According to the one it is there said, “Make me whole for Thy mercy’s sake;” according to this other it is here said, “Judge me, O Lord, according to my righteousness.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For there he asks for a remedy to escape from disease; but here for protection from falling into disease.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: According to the former it is said, “Make me whole, O Lord, according to Thy mercy:” according to the latter it is said, “My righteous help is from the Lord, who maketh whole the upright in heart.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Both the one and the other maketh men whole; but the former removes them from sickness into health, the latter preserves them in this health.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Therefore there the help is merciful, because the sinner hath no desert, who as yet longeth to be justified, “believing on Him who justifieth the ungodly;” but here the help is righteous, because it is given to one already righteous.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Let the sinner then who said, “I am weak,” say in the first place, “Make me whole, O Lord, for Thy mercy’s sake;” and here let the righteous man, who said, “If I have repaid them that recompense me evil,” say, “My righteous help is from the Lord, who maketh whole the upright in heart.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For if he sets forth the medicine, by which we may be healed when weak, how much more that by which we may be kept in health.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For if “while we were yet sinners, Christ died for us, how much more being now justified shall we be kept whole from wrath through Him.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “My righteous help is from the Lord, who maketh whole the upright in heart.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: God, who searcheth the hearts and reins, directeth the righteous; but with righteous help maketh He whole the upright in heart.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: He doth not as He searcheth the hearts and reins, so make whole the upright in heart and reins; for the thoughts are both bad in a depraved heart, and good in an upright heart; but delights which are not good belong to the reins, for they are more low and earthly; but those that are good not to the reins, but to the heart itself.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Wherefore men cannot be so called upright in reins, as they are called upright in heart, since where the thought is, there at once the delight is too; which cannot be, unless when things divine and eternal are thought of.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Thou hast given,” he says, “joy in my heart,” when he had said, “The light of Thy countenance has been stamped on us, O Lord.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For although the phantoms of things temporal, which the mind falsely pictures to itself, when tossed by vain and mortal hope, to vain imagination oftentimes bring a delirious and maddened joy; yet this delight must be attributed not to the heart, but to the reins; for all these imaginations have been drawn from lower, that is, earthly and carnal things.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Hence it comes, that God, who searcheth the hearts and reins, and perceiveth in the heart upright thoughts, in the reins no delights, affordeth righteous help to the upright in heart, where heavenly delights are coupled with clean thoughts.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And therefore when in another Psalm he had said, “Moreover even to-night my reins have chided me;” he went on to say as touching help, “I foresaw the Lord alway in my sight, for He is on my right hand, that I should not be moved.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Where he shows that he suffered suggestions only from the reins, not delights as well; for he had suffered these, then he would of course be moved.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But he said, “The Lord is on my right hand, that I should not be moved;” and then he adds, “Wherefore was my heart delighted;” that the reins should have been able to chide, not delight him.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: The delight accordingly was produced not in the reins, but there, where against the chiding of the reins God was foreseen to be on the right hand, that is, in the heart.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “God the righteous judge, strong (in endurance) and long-suffering” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: What God is judge, but the Lord, who judgeth the people?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: He is righteous; who “shall render to every man according to his works.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: He is strong (in endurance); who, being most powerful, for our salvation bore even with ungodly persecutors.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: He is long-suffering; who did not immediately, after His resurrection, hurry away to punishment, even those that persecuted Him, but bore with them, that they might at length turn from that ungodliness to salvation: and still He beareth with them, reserving the last penalty for the last judgment, and up to this present time inviting sinners to repentance.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Not bringing in anger every day.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Perhaps “bringing in anger” is a more significant expression than being angry (and so we find it in the Greek copies); that the anger, whereby He punisheth, should not be in Him, but in the minds of those ministers who obey the commandments of truth through whom orders are given even to the lower ministries, who are called angels of wrath, to punish sin: whom even now the punishment of men delights not for justice’ sake, in which they have no pleasure, but for malice’ sake.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: God then doth not “bring in anger every day,” that is, He doth not collect His ministers for vengeance every day.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For now the patience of God inviteth to repentance: but in the last time, when men “through their hardness and impenitent heart shall have treasured up for themselves anger in the day of anger, and revelation of the righteous judgment of God, then He will brandish His sword.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Unless ye be converted,” He says, “He will brandish His sword” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: The Lord Man Himself may be taken to be God’s double-edged sword, that is, His spear, which at His first coming He will not brandish, but hideth as it were in the sheath of humiliation: but He will brandish it, when at the second coming to judge the quick and dead, in the manifest splendour of His glory, He shall flash light on His righteous ones, and terror on the ungodly.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For in other copies, instead of, “He shall brandish His sword,” it has been written, “He shall make bright His spear:” by which word I think the last coming of the Lord’s glory most appropriately signified: seeing that is understood of His person, which another Psalm has, “Deliver, O Lord, my soul from the ungodly, Thy spear from the enemies of Thine hand.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: He hath bent His bow, and made it ready.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: The tenses of the words must not be altogether overlooked, how he has spoken of “the sword” in the future, “He will brandish;” of “the bow” in the past, “He hath bent:” and these words of the past tense follow after.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “And in it He hath prepared the instruments of death: He hath wrought His arrows for the burning” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: That bow then I would readily take to be the Holy Scripture, in which by the strength of the New Testament, as by a sort of string, the hardness of the Old has been bent and subdued.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: From thence the Apostles are sent forth like arrows, or divine preachings are shot.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Which arrows “He has wrought for the burning,” arrows, that is, whereby being stricken they might be inflamed with heavenly love.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For by what other arrows was she stricken, who saith, “Bring me into the house of wine, place me among perfumes, crowd me among honey, for I have been wounded with love”?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: By what other arrows is he kindled, who, desirous of returning to God, and coming back from wandering, asketh for help against crafty tongues, and to whom it is said, “What shall be given thee, or what added to thee against the crafty tongue?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Sharp arrows of the mighty, with devastating coals:” that is, coals, whereby, when thou art stricken and set on fire, thou mayest burn with so great love of the kingdom of heaven, as to despise the tongues of all that resist thee, and would recall thee from thy purpose, and to deride their persecutions, saying, “Who shall separate me from the love of Christ? shall tribulation, or distress, or persecution, or famine, or nakedness, or peril, or sword?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For I am persuaded,” he says, “that neither death, nor life, nor angel, nor principality, nor things present, not things to come, nor power, nor height, nor depth, nor other creature, shall be able to separate me from the love of God, which is in Christ Jesus our Lord.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Thus for the burning hath He wrought His arrows.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For in the Greek copies it is found thus, “He hath wrought His arrows for the burning.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But most of the Latin copies have “burning arrows.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But whether the arrows themselves burn, or make others burn, which of course they cannot do unless they burn themselves, the sense is complete.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But since he has said that the Lord has prepared not arrows only, but “instruments of death” too, in the bow, it may be asked, what are “instruments of death”?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Are they, per-adventure, heretics?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For they too, out of the same bow, that is, out of the same Scriptures, light upon souls not to be inflamed with love but destroyed with poison: which does not happen but after their deserts: wherefore even this dispensation is to be assigned to the Divine Providence, not that it makes men sinners, but that it orders them after they have sinned.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For through sin reaching them with an ill purpose, they are forced to understand them ill, that this should be itself the punishment of sin: by whose death, nevertheless, the sons of the Catholic Church are, as it were by certain thorns, so to say, aroused from slumber, and make progress toward the understanding of the holy Scriptures.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “For there must be also heresies, that they which are approved,” he says, “may be made manifest among you:” that is, among men, seeing they are manifest to God.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Or has He haply ordained the same arrows to be at once instruments of death for the destruction of unbelievers, and wrought them burning, or for the burning, for the exercising of the faithful?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For that is not false that the Apostle says, “To the one we are the savour of life unto life, to the other the savour of death unto death; and who is sufficient for these things?”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: It is no wonder then if the same Apostles be both instruments of death in those from whom they suffered persecution, and fiery arrows to inflame the hearts of believers.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Now after this dispensation righteous judgment will come: of which the Psalmist so speaks, as that we may understand that each man’s punishment is wrought out of his own sin, and his iniquity turned into vengeance: that we may not suppose that that tranquillity and ineffable light of God brings forth from Itself the means of punishing sin; but that it so ordereth sins, that what have been delights to man in sinning, should be instruments to the Lord avenging.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Behold,” he says, “he hath travailed with injustice.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Now what had he conceived, that he should travail with injustice?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “He hath conceived,” he says, “toil.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Hence then comes that, “In toil shall thou eat thy bread.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Hence too that, “Come unto Me all ye that toil and are heavy laden; for My yoke is easy, and My burden light.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For toil will never cease, except one love that which cannot be taken away against his will.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For when those things are loved which we can lose against our will, we must needs toil for them most miserably; and to obtain them, amid the straitnesses of earthly cares, whilst each desires to snatch them for himself, and to be beforehand with another, or to wrest it from him, must scheme injustice.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Duly then, and quite in order, hath he travailed with injustice, who has conceived toil.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Now he bringeth forth what, save that with which he hath travailed, although he has not travailed with that which he conceived?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For that is not born, which is not conceived; but seed is conceived, that which is formed from the seed is born.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Toil is then the seed of iniquity, but sin the conception of toil, that is, that first sin, to “depart from God.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: He then hath travailed with injustice, who hath conceived toil.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “And he hath brought forth iniquity.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Iniquity” is the same as “injustice:” he hath brought forth then that with which he travailed.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: What follows next?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “He hath opened a ditch, and digged it” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: To open a ditch is, in earthly matters, that is, as it were in the earth, to prepare deceit, that another fall therein, whom the unrighteous man wishes to deceive.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Now this ditch is opened when consent is given to the evil suggestion of earthly lusts: but it is digged when after consent we press on to actual work of deceit.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But how can it be, that iniquity should rather hurt the righteous man against whom it proceeds, than the unrighteous heart whence it proceeds?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Accordingly, the stealer of money, for instance, while he desires to inflict painful harm upon another, is himself maimed by the wound of avarice.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Now who, even out of his right mind, sees not how great is the difference between these men, when one suffers the loss of money, the other of innocence?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “He will fall” then “into the pit which he hath made.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: As it is said in another Psalm, “The Lord is known in executing judgments; the sinner is caught in the works of his own hands.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “His toil shall be turned on his head, and his iniquity shall descend on his pate” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For he had no mind to escape sin: but was brought under sin as a slave, so to say, as the Lord saith, “Whosoever sinneth is a slave.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: His iniquity then will be upon him, when he is subject to his iniquity; for he could not say to the Lord, what the innocent and upright say, “My glory, and the lifter up of my head.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: He then will be in such wise below, as that his iniquity may be above, and descend on him; for that it weigheth him down and burdens him, and suffers him not to fly back to the rest of the saints.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: This occurs, when in an ill regulated man reason is a slave, and lust hath dominion.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “I will confess to the Lord according to His justice” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: This is not the sinner’s confession: for he says this, who said above most truly, “If there be iniquity in my hands:” but it is a confession of God’s justice, in which we speak thus, Verily, O Lord, Thou art just, in that Thou both so protectest the just, that Thou enlightenest them by Thyself; and so orderest sinners, that they be punished not by Thine, but by their own malice.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: This confession so praises the Lord, that the blasphemies of the ungodly can avail nothing, who, willing to excuse their evil deeds, are unwilling to attribute to their own fault that they sin, that is, are unwilling to attribute their fault to their fault.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Accordingly they find either fortune or fate to accuse, or the devil, to whom He who made us hath willed that it should be in our power to refuse consent: or they bring in another nature, which is not of God: wretched waverers, and erring, rather than confessing to God, that He should pardon them.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For it is not fit that any be pardoned, except he says, I have sinned.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: He, then, that sees the deserts of souls so ordered by God, that while each has his own given him, the fair beauty of the universe is in no part violated, in all things praises God: and this is not the confession of sinners, but of the righteous.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For it is not the sinner’s confession when the Lord says, “I confess to Thee, O Lord of heaven and earth, because Thou hast hid these things from the wise, and revealed them to babes.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Likewise in Ecclesiasticus it is said, “Confess to the Lord in all His works: and in confession ye shall say this, All the works of the Lord are exceeding good.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Which can be seen in this Psalm, if any one with a pious mind, by the Lord’s help, distinguish between the rewards of the righteous and the penalties of the sinners, how that in these two the whole creation, which God made and rules, is adorned with a beauty wondrous and known to few.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Thus then he says, “I will confess to the Lord according to His justice,” as one who saw that darkness was not made by God, but ordered nevertheless.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For God said, “Let light be made, and light was made.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: He did not say, Let darkness be made, and darkness was made: and yet He ordered it.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And therefore it is said, “God divided between the light, and the darkness: and God called the light day, and the darkness He called night.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: This is the distinction, He made the one and ordered it: but the other He made not, but yet He ordered this too.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But now that sins are signified by darkness, so is it seen in the Prophet, who says, “And thy darkness shall be as the noon day:” and in the Apostle, who says, “He that hateth his brother is in darkness:” and above all that text, “Let us cast off the works of darkness, and let us put on the armour of light.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Not that there is any nature of darkness.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For all nature, in so far as it is nature, is compelled to be.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Now being belongs to light: not being to darkness.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: He then that leaves Him by whom he was made, and inclines to that whence he was made, that is, to nothing, is in this sin endarkened: and yet he does not utterly perish, but he is ordered among the lowest things.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Therefore after the Psalmist said, “I will confess unto the Lord:” that we might not understand it of confession of sins, he adds lastly, “And I will sing to the name of the Lord most high.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Now singing has relation to joy, but repentance of sins to sadness.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: This Psalm can also be taken in the person of the Lord Man: if only that which is there spoken in humiliation be referred to our weakness, which He bore.
